titleStmt.title: Letter from Matthew Nathan to the Treasury, 3 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Matthew Nathan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), the under-secretary for Ireland, to the Treasury. Nathan served as under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan, and his chief secretary Augustine Birrell (1850-1933), each offered their resignations in the aftermath of the Easter Rising.This letter concerns the collapse of a wall which stood as a boundary between the North Circular Laundry and Mountjoy Prison. The Laundry had claimed that the prison authorities were responsible for the collapse of the wall as they had allowed a heavy growth of ivy on the prison side. Although the prison Board has denied liability Nathan proposes the sanctioning of a payment of not more than £50 towards the construction of a new wall.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/3719a

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 3719 3rd March, 1916. Sir, I am directed by the Lord Lieutenant to state for the
information of the Lords Commissioners of His Majesty's
Treasury that during a storm in Dublin on the night of the
12th November last a portion of the wall, 15 feet high,formâ
ing the boundary between the grounds attached to the Medical
Officer's quarters at Mountjoy Prison and those of the North
Circular Laundry Company was entirely blown down for a length
of 24 yards. The Laundry Company have preferred a claim against the
General Prisons Board for a contribution towards the cost
of rebuilding the portion of the wall blown down alleging
that the damage was caused in some measure by the growth of
ivy on the prison side of the wall. The Board report that the deeds in their possession
show quite clearly that the wall in question was in existence
when the prison was built and in this connection I enclose
for Their Lordships information a copy of a report made to
the Board by Mr Abraham, Clerk of Works, together with a
map showing the position of the wall in question. The Board
presume, however, that the Company are under no obligation
to build a high wall such as that which was blown down or
any wall at all; and should the Company not do so the Board
would have to build an entirely new wall the cost of which
at
The Secretary,
Treasury,
London, S.W.
at the present price of materials might amount to nearly
Â£100. His Excellency is advised that the Prison Authorities
are not liable for the fall of the wall, but that the Board's
view of the situation as stated above is accurate; and in
circumstances I am to recommend Their Lordships to
sanction a contribution of not more than Â£50 from the Vote
for Prisons, Ireland, towards the construction of a new wall. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, (Sd) Matthew Nathan
